title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A coronary angioplasty procedure is also known as percutaneous coronary intervention.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The procedure is done on blood vessels called coronary arteries.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: They supply your heart muscle with oxygen.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The goal is to restore blood flow if a substance called plaque has significantly narrowed

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: these vessels.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: To begin the procedure, the doctor will numb the skin in your wrist.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A needle will be placed through your skin and into your radial artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Next, a flexible guide wire will be passed through the needle into your artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Then, the needle will be withdrawn.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: It will be exchanged for a small, flexible tube called a sheath.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: This permits access into your artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: You may feel pressure when the doctor inserts the sheath, but you will not feel it moving

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: inside your artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Next, the guide wire will be advanced up to your heart.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A flexible tube called a catheter will be advanced over the wire to your coronary arteries.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The progress of the procedure will be checked with an x-ray device called a flora scope.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: At this point, your doctor will remove the guide wire.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Then, the doctor will move the tip of the catheter just inside the coronary artery to be examined.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A special die will be injected into the artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: This allows your doctor to view it better with a flora scope.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The die will make any blockages in the artery stand out.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: If a significant blockage is found, your doctor will insert a guide wire into the artery.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A balloon on the tip of the catheter will be moved along the wire to the blockage.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: When the balloon inflates, it will expand the artery and improve the blood flow.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: You may feel some chest discomfort while this is happening.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: After this, your doctor will deflate and remove the balloon.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: A wire mesh tube called a stent may be placed in the treated area.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The stent helps keep the coronary artery open.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: Your doctor will choose the proper size stent, which is compressed over a balloon.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: The stent will be moved into the artery over the same guide wire.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: When the balloon is inflated, the stent will expand and lock into place.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: After the balloon catheter is taken out, the stent will stay in place to hold the artery open.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: At the end of the procedure, the guide wire will be removed.

title: ANCE00174 Coronary Artery Angioplasty (Radial Access)
text_en: To find out more about coronary artery angioplasty, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Coronary artery angiography is an x-ray test of the heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: This test can view blood vessels in your heart called coronary arteries.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: It can find out if a waxy substance called plaque has narrowed these vessels.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: And, it can show how well the valves and chambers of your heart are working.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Coronary angiography is often done with a medical procedure called cardiac catheterization.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Before the procedure, you will be placed on a table with an x-ray device called a florescope.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Usually cardiac catheterization takes place through the radial artery in your wrist.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: This route normally has less bleeding and complications.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Sometimes the femoral artery in your groin may be used instead.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: To begin, the doctor will numb the skin in your wrist.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: A needle will be inserted into your radial artery.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: A wire will be passed through this needle.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The wire will be gently guided through your arteries to your heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The needle will be taken out and replaced with a small flexible tube called a sheath.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: This permits access to your radial artery.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Next, a soft flexible tube called a catheter will be slipped over the wire through the sheath.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The catheter will be threaded up to your heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Then, the florescope will be moved around your chest to take x-ray images of your heart.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Your doctor will use the images to check on the catheter.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: You may feel pressure as the wire and sheath are put in, but you will not feel them inside your arteries.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Once the catheter reaches your coronary arteries, the wire will be removed.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The catheter tip will be placed just inside each artery to inject a special die.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: This die allows the florescope to take x-ray pictures of your arteries, called angiograms.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Any blockages will be clearly identified as the arteries fill with die.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: You may feel flushed or slightly nauseated when the die is injected.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: At the end of the procedure, the catheter will be removed through your wrist.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: A pressure band will be used over the radial artery to prevent bleeding.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: Then, the sheath will be removed.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: The pressure band will remain on your wrist until bleeding has stopped.

title: ANCE00182 Coronary Artery Angiography (Cardiac Catheterization)
text_en: To find out more about coronary angiography, talk to your healthcare provider.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: If you or someone you know has recently had a coronary artery bypass graft or cabbage,

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: this video will help you understand the steps of the procedure and how it restores blood flow to your heart.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A coronary artery bypass graft or cabbage is performed to improve circulation to the heart muscle.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: In this procedure a healthy artery or vein from another part of the body is connected or grafted to the blocked coronary artery.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The grafted artery or vein bypasses the blocked portion of the coronary artery, carrying oxygen-rich blood to the heart muscle.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: One or more coronary arteries may be bypassed during a single operation.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Before the surgery, an intravenous line will be started and you may be given a medication to help you relax.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Cabbage procedures are done under general anesthesia, which will put you to sleep for the duration of the operation.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A breathing tube will be inserted through your mouth and into your throat to help you breathe.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A catheter will be placed in your bladder to drain your urine.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Coronary artery bypass surgery generally takes 3 to 6 hours.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: During a conventional bypass surgery, your surgeon begins by making an incision in the skin over your breastbone or sternum.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: He or she will then cut the sternum and move your ribcage in order to get to your heart.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Throughout the procedure, your circulatory system may be connected to a cardiopulmonary bypass pump or heart lung machine.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: This machine may temporarily perform the functions of your heart and lungs during the surgery, allowing your heart to be stopped if desired while the surgeon shows the grafts into place.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Different blood vessels may typically be used for the grafts.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The internal thoracic arteries in the chest, also known as internal mammary arteries, or the saffinous veins in the legs.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: For the internal thoracic artery graft, your surgeon will leave the upper end attached to the subclavian artery and divert the lower end from your chest wall to your coronary artery just beyond the blockage.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Your surgeon will then sew the graft into place.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: For the saffinous vein graft, your surgeon will suture one end to the aorta and the other end to the narrowed artery just beyond the blockage.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: With the grafts securely in place, your surgeon may use electrical signals to restore the heart beat and attach a temporary pacemaker to the heart.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Once your heart is again beating normally, if the surgeon used the heart lung machine, it would be disconnected.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Your surgeon will wire the breastbone back together and suture the skin incision closed.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A temporary drainage tube will be placed through the skin beneath the incision.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: When the surgeon chooses to do the surgery without using the heart lung machine, the heart continues to beat.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: This may be referred to as off-pump bypass surgery or minimally invasive surgery.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Instead, a mechanical device is used to steady the part of the heart where the grafting is being done.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Surgeons perform minimally invasive bypass procedures using specially designed instruments inserted through small incisions or ports in the chest.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: After surgery, you will be taken to the intensive care unit.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The activity of your heart will be carefully monitored.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: If necessary, pacing wires will be used to temporarily control your heart rate.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The chest tube will remain in place to drain excess blood and air from the chest cavity.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Once you can breathe on your own, your breathing tube will be removed and replaced with an oxygen mask.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The bladder catheter will remain in place.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: As you recover from surgery over the next three to five days, all of these devices will be gradually removed.

title: ANCE00199 Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: Upon discharge from your procedure, be sure to contact your doctor if you have any questions or experience any side effects from the medications you are taking.

file: ANCE00199S101_What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S101 What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: A coronary artery bypass graft, or cabbage, is performed to improve circulation to the heart muscle.

file: ANCE00199S101_What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S101 What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: In this procedure, a healthy artery or vein from another part of the body is connected or grafted to the blocked coronary artery.

file: ANCE00199S101_What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S101 What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: The grafted artery or vein bypasses the blocked portion of the coronary artery, carrying oxygen-rich blood to the heart muscle.

file: ANCE00199S101_What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S101 What is a Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG)
text_en: One or more coronary arteries may be bypassed during a single operation.

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: Different blood vessels may typically be used for the grafts.

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: The internal thoracic arteries in the chest, also known as internal mammary arteries,

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: or the saffonous veins in the legs.

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: For the internal thoracic artery graft, your surgeon will leave the upper end attached to the subclavian artery,

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: and divert the lower end from your chest wall to your coronary artery, just beyond the blockage.

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: Your surgeon will then sew the graft into place.

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: For the saffonous vein graft, your surgeon will suture one end to the aorta,

file: ANCE00199S102_What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S102 What Blood Vessels are Commonly Used as a Graft for a Heart Bypass
text_en: and the other end to the narrowed artery, just beyond the blockage.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: During a conventional bypass surgery, your surgeon begins by making an incision in the skin over your breastbone or sternum.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: He or she will then cut the sternum and move your ribcage in order to get to your heart.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: Throughout the procedure, your circulatory system may be connected to a cardiopulmonary bypass pump or heart lung machine.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: When the surgeon chooses to do the surgery without using the heart lung machine, the heart continues to beat.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: This may be referred to as off-pump bypass surgery or minimally invasive surgery.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: Instead, a mechanical device is used to steady the part of the heart where the grafting is being done.

file: ANCE00199S103_What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures_.mp4
title: ANCE00199S103 What are the Different Types of Coronary Artery Bypass Graft (CABG) Procedures
text_en: Surgeons perform minimally invasive bypass procedures using specially designed instruments inserted through small incisions or ports in the chest.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Please watch this entire video before caring for your drain.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: This video will teach you how to take care of your surgical drain.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Curgical drains remove fluid from under your skin near your surgical site.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: This helps prevent infection and encourages healing.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: A surgical drain consists of a drain tube that runs from your surgical site

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: to a bulb that collects the fluid.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You strip the drain tube to keep it clear and remove any clots or blockages.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Then you empty the bulb when it is half full or as instructed by your healthcare provider.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You will need alcohol wipes, a measuring cup and a drainage record sheet.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Be sure to wear disposable gloves if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Be careful not to pull on the tubing.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You should not feel any tugging where the tube enters your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 1. Wash your hands with soap and water and then dry them.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 2. Put on disposable gloves if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 3. Look for clots or blockages that may prevent the fluid from flowing out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Some clots may be hidden inside the tube under your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 4. Loosen the clots by gently squeezing the tubes surrounding them.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 5. Use one hand to hold the drain tube in place where it leaves your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 6. Use your other hand to pinch the tube with an alcohol wipe between your finger and thumb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 7. Slide your pinched fingers along the tube to force any fluid out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: You may need to repeat steps 5 through 7 several times to clear the tube.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Try not to let go of the tube between steps.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: If fluid remains in the tube or you accidentally let go, repeat steps 5 through 7 using a new alcohol wipe.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Do not allow the bulb to become more than half full.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Too much fluid in the bulb reduces its ability to remove fluid from underneath your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Now you will begin emptying the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 8. Hold the bulb lower than your incision so that fluid moves out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 9. Point the bulb away from your body.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Never squeeze the bulb before taking the cap off.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 10. Remove the cap.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Never touch the opening with your bare hands.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 11. Hold the measuring cup under the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 12. Turn the bulb upside down and squeeze the fluid into the cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 13. After removing the fluid, continue squeezing the bulb and use a new alcohol wipe to clean the top.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 14. While still squeezing the bulb, put the cap back on the top.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: The depressed bulb creates suction that continuously removes fluid from underneath your skin.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 15. Read the amount of fluid in the measuring cup.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 16. Write the amount on your record sheet.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Step 17. Empty and rinse the cup as directed.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Keep the bulb below the level of your incision to help the fluid move out of the tube and into the bulb.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice the amount of fluid suddenly increases or decreases, the odor of the fluid changes.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: The fluid contains pus or becomes thicker over time.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Your drain tube falls out or your incision opens.

title: AND12001 Drainage Tube Care
text_en: Your incision is red, swollen, painful or has pus coming out or your temperature is 101 degrees Fahrenheit or higher.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Look at your foot and toes on the leg recovering from the surgical procedure.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Compare your feet. Do you see any differences?

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Your foot and toes on the surgical side should feel warm, be normal in color, and have little to no swelling.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: These signs mean you have good blood flow in your leg.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: If your foot looks red or blue, feels cool, or has a lot of swelling, your leg may have poor blood flow.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Does your foot feel numb or tingly?

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: A feeling of numbness and tingling may also be a sign of poor blood flow.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Or, you may feel numbness from a type of anesthesia called a nerve block until it wears off.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: If you are not sure if you had a nerve block, ask your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 3. Wiggle your toes. Wiggling your toes may improve blood flow to your foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 through 3 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Another way to check for good blood flow is by doing a capillary refill test.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Capillaries are small blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood to your toes.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: The capillary refill test allows you to see how well blood is flowing through these blood vessels to your toes on the surgical side.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Gently pinch the tip of your toe directly over the toenail with your hand.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Squeeze until your toenail turns white. Then let go.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Your toenail should turn pink again within 2-3 seconds.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: This sign means that you have good blood flow in your leg and foot.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps 1 and 2 every 4 hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice. Your foot looks red or blue.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Your foot looks very swollen. Your foot feels cold, numb or tingly, unless you had a nerve block during surgery, making your foot feel numb until it wears off.

title: AND12002 Circulation Checks  Lower Extremity
text_en: Or, your toenail takes more than 2-3 seconds to return to a pink color when doing the capillary refill test.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Please watch this entire video before checking the blood flow in your arm.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: This video will teach you how to check the blood flow in your arm.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Good blood flow is important for recovery after the surgical procedure on your arm.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Look at your hand and fingers on the arm recovering from the surgical procedure.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Compare your hands. Do you see any differences?

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Your hand and fingers on the surgical side should feel warm, be normal in color, and have little to no swelling.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: These signs mean you have good blood flow in your arm.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: If your hand looks red or blue, feels cool, or has a lot of swelling, your arm may have poor blood flow.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Does your hand feel numb and tingly?

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: A feeling of numbness and tingling can also be a sign of poor blood flow.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Or, you may feel numbness from a type of anesthesia called a nerve block until it wears off.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: If you are not sure if you had a nerve block, ask your surgeon.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 3. Wiggle your fingers. Wiggling your fingers may improve blood flow to your hand.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps one through three every four hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Another way to check for good blood flow is by doing a capillary refill test.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Capillaries are small blood vessels that carry oxygen-rich blood to your fingers.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: The capillary refill test allows you to see how well blood is flowing through these blood vessels to your fingers on the surgical side.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 1. Gently pinch the tip of your finger directly over the fingernail with your other hand.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Step 2. Squeeze until your fingernail turns white. Then let go.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Your fingernail should turn pink again within two to three seconds.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: This sign means that you have good blood flow in your arm and hand.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Repeat steps one and two every four hours, or as directed by your surgeon.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Contact your surgeon if you notice. Your hand looks red or blue. Your hand looks very swollen.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Your hand feels cold, numb or tingly, unless you had a nerve block during surgery making your arm feel numb until it wears off.

title: AND12003 Circulation Checks  Upper Extremity
text_en: Or your fingernail takes more than two to three seconds to return to a pink color when doing the capillary refill test.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Please watch the entire video before caring for your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: This video will teach you how to care for your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: An incision is a cut made in your skin during an operation.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: The edges of your incision may be held together with stitches, staples, skin glue, or skin

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: closure strips.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: A bandage called addressing covers your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: To prevent skin infections, keep your dressing clean and dry.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Taking good care of your incision will help it heal more quickly.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Caring for your incision involves cleaning your incision and changing your dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: You will need New bandages A roll of medical tape

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Clean scissors Two pairs of disposable gloves

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Two clean, soft cloths or paper towels A disposable plastic bag

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: And a waste basket.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Keep these materials away from your incision to reduce your risk of infection.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Prepare materials

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 1.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wash your hands with soap and water for 30 seconds and then dry them with a clean towel or paper towels.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Clean hands are the best protection against spreading infection.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 2.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Open a new bandage wrapper.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Leave the bandage inside the wrapper and be careful not to touch it.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 3.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Cut four pieces of tape and hang them from the edge of a nearby table or counter.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: The pieces of tape should be the same length as the old ones around your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Remove old dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 1.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wash your hands again with soap and water and then dry them.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 2.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Put on disposable gloves.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 3.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: With one hand, press down gently on the skin next to the old tape around your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: With the other hand, carefully peel away the old tape toward your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 4.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: After loosening all of the tape, remove your old dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Do not remove any skin closure strips covering your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Let them fall off on their own in about 5-10 days.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: If your dressing sticks to your incision, don't tear it off.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Tearing it off may cause bleeding and delay healing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Moisten the dressing with warm water.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wait for a minute or two until you can easily remove it.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Look at your old dressing after you remove it.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: If the dressing has a foul or sweet smell or it has yellow or greenish stain, call your

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: healthcare provider.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 5.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Place the old dressing in a disposable plastic bag.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 6.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Remove the disposable gloves.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Throw the gloves and the plastic bag containing your old dressing in the waste basket.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Clean the incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Clean your incision only if your healthcare provider says you should.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Your healthcare provider may only want you to change the dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Make sure you understand what your healthcare provider wants you to do and be sure to call

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: with questions or concerns.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 1.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wash your hands with soap and water and dry them.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 2.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Put on a new pair of disposable gloves.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 3.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Always use a clean cloth, warm water, and if your healthcare provider says you can, a mild

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: antibacterial soap to gently remove dried material from the area around your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Start with the area's nearest urinsision and wipe outwards or away from the incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Use a different part of the cloth each time you wipe your skin.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: When the cloth is dirty, use a new clean cloth.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Do not scrub or remove any scabs.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Scabs protect your incision while it heals.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Allow them to fall off on their own.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Do not wipe or rub over stitches, staples, or skin closure strips.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Unless your healthcare provider says you should, do not clean your incision with alcohol,

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: iodine, or peroxide.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 4.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Let your incision air dry.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: As it dries, check your incision for the following.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Stitches or staples that are out of place.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: The edges of your incision pulling open, bleeding around your incision, redness that extends

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: more than one inch from your incision edges, increased skin warmth around your incision,

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: swelling around your incision, foul or sweet smelling incision odor, or a white, yellow,

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: or green discharge coming from your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Call your healthcare provider if you notice any of these changes.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Unless your healthcare provider says you should, do not use, appointment, lotion, or powder

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: on your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 5.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Remove the disposable gloves and throw them away in the waste basket.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Apply new dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 1.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wash your hands with soap and water and dry them.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 2.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Put on a new pair of disposable gloves.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 3.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Pick up a gauze pad by one corner.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Do not touch the side that will be next to your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Carefully cover your incision with the gauze pad.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: You may need more than one gauze pad if fluid is still coming out of your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 4.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Hold the gauze pad in place with one hand and tape the gauze pad to your skin with the

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: other hand.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Make sure that you seal all four edges of the dressing.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: If your skin is red from the tape on your old dressing, put the new tape on a different

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: part of your skin.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: If your skin is swollen or has blisters, contact your healthcare provider.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: You may be allergic to the tape and need to try a different kind.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 5.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Put your gloves and put them into the waste basket.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Step 6.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Wash your hands again with soap and water and dry them.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: It's normal to see mild redness and swelling around your incision edges or a clear or pinkish

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: discharge.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: However, you should contact your healthcare provider if you notice.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Any white, yellow or green discharge coming from your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: A foul or sweet incision odor, the shape of your incision changes.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Redness that extends more than one inch from your incision edges, increased warmth of the

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: skin around your incision, and bleeding or swelling around your incision.

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: Also call your healthcare provider if you have nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or a temperature

title: AND13008 Incision Care Discharge Instructions
text_en: of 101 degrees Fahrenheit or higher.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Please watch this entire video before removing your pump.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: This video will teach you how to tell if your pump is working, how to care for it, and how to remove it.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: A pain pump is used after surgery to deliver numbing medicine directly to the area that was operated on.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The medicine numbs the nerves near your incision, so you have less pain.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The medicine continuously flows very slowly out of the pump through a small tube placed under the skin near your surgical incision.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The small tube, called a catheter, connects to the pump.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: And a clamp. And filter are attached along the tube.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: You can wear a small pouch to hold the pump so you can go about your daily activities.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The pump gives you numbing medicine at a rate ordered by your surgeon. The rate on the pump cannot be changed.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: You receive the medicine over two to four days.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: When the pump is empty, you remove the tube and throw the pump and tube away.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: How to tell if your pain pump is working?

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The pump delivers medicine so slowly that you cannot see it moving in the tube.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: It may take 24 to 48 hours before you notice a change in the size and look of the ball.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: You can tell when the pump is working if your surgical site feels numb and the medicine reduces your pain.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The ball gradually gets smaller and smaller.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: And the outside bag on the pump gets looser and begins to wrinkle.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The pump is empty when the bag looks flat and you can feel a hard tube in the middle. This usually takes 48 to 72 hours.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Carrying for your pain pump.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Check your pain pump several times a day to make sure.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: The clamp is open and slides easily. Nothing is covering the filter.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: And there are no kinks or bends in the tubing.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: If you notice the pump or tube is leaking, close the clamp and call your surgeon.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: If the tube separates from the pump, do not reconnect it.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Close the clamp and call your surgeon.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: If the tube comes out of your skin, do not put it back in.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Close the clamp and call your surgeon.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: You should also close the clamp on the tube and call your surgeon if you notice, ringing or buzzing in your ears.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Blurred vision. A metal taste in your mouth.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Numbness or tingling in your fingers, toes or around your mouth.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Feel drowsy or confused.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Increased pain.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Fever chills or sweats.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Trouble breathing.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Redness, swelling, warmth or pain around the site where the tube enters your skin.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Bleeding or discharge from the tube site.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Feel dizzy or lightheaded.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Make sure to protect your pump, filter and tube from water.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Do not take a tub bath or put your pump underwater and be sure to follow your surgeon's instructions about showering.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: When sleeping, keep the pump inside of the small pouch and place it next to you on top of the covers.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: This helps keep the tube loose and in place.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Do not put the pump under the covers on the floor or hang it from anything.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Materials for removing the pump.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: To remove your pain pump you will need an adhesive bandage and a trash bag.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Removing the pump.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 1. Wash your hands with soap and water and then dry them.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 2. Gently loosen the bandage around the site where the tube enters your skin.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Remove it by slowly peeling it back.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 3. Remove the tape used to hold the tube in place.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 4. Hold the tube close to the skin.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Gently pull the tube out at a 90 degree angle.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Do not tug or pull hard. The tube should come out easily.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: If it feels hard to remove or stretches, stop and call your surgeon.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Continued pulling could break the tube.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 5. After the tube comes out, check the tip for a black mark to make sure you removed the entire tube.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Call your surgeon if you do not see the black mark.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 6. Cover the site where the tube entered your skin with an adhesive bandage.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 7. Throw away the tube, pump and bandages into a trash bag.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Checking the tube entry site the next day.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 1. Wash your hands again and then dry them.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: Step 2. Remove the adhesive bandage the next day and look at the site.

title: AND13015 Removing Your Pain Pump
text_en: If you have redness, pain or warmth at the site, call your surgeon.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: This video will help you understand a heart attack and how to manage it.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Please watch the entire video to learn how to manage your recovery.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: If you have been hospitalized with a heart attack, it's important to recover properly when

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: you return home.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: A heart attack, also called a myocardial infarction, or MI, occurs when blood flow to an area of

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: your heart is greatly reduced.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: When the blood supply is not able to keep up with the demand, the heart muscle cells don't

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: get enough blood to stay alive.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: The result is death of heart muscle cells in the affected area of the heart.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: The goals after a heart attack are to keep your heart healthy and avoid another heart attack.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Recovery takes time and the amount is different for each patient.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: It depends upon how much of your heart muscle is damaged and your individual rate of recovery.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: You may also have had a procedure.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: This may also affect your recovery time.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: You can lower your risk of having another heart attack by taking these steps, follow your

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: doctor's instructions, make some lifestyle changes, and know when to seek medical attention.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Following the instructions of your doctor and healthcare team is very important to your recovery.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: This includes taking your medications exactly as prescribed by your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Don't stop taking your medications or skip doses.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Take note of any side effects and tell your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Don't take any over-the-counter medication or supplement without first consulting your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Tell your doctor about any other prescriptions you're taking.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Some medications can prevent your heart medicine from working.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: It's important that your recovery is monitored.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Keep all of your follow-up appointments.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Have exams done as directed by your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: For example, if your doctor has told you to check your blood pressure regularly, do

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: as directed.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Lowering your risk of another heart attack usually requires some lifestyle changes.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Limit your salt and sodium intake by doing the following.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Don't add salt when you cook or to food at the table.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Avoid processed and fast foods.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Compare food labels and choose the items that are lower in salt and sodium.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Avoid a heart-healthy diet, including low fat, low cholesterol, and high fiber foods.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: A heart-healthy diet helps you maintain or achieve a good blood pressure and weight.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: It also helps prevent other conditions that may further stress your heart.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Limit your alcohol intake.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Risk your doctor if it's safe to include any alcohol in your diet.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Maintain a healthy weight.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Don't smoke.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Avoid all tobacco products and secondhand smoke.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Nicotine increases heart rate and blood pressure.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: At the same time, it decreases the amount of oxygen-rich blood delivered to your body's

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: tissues.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Allow yourself time to heal by taking it easy for the first four weeks.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Getting a good night's sleep.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Rest in the afternoon.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Avoid heavy lifting and gradually increasing your physical activity.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Ask your doctor when you can resume driving, sexual activity, and return to work.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: For weeks after your heart attack, you will begin cardiac rehab, which includes a closely

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: monitored exercise regimen.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: After completing the cardiac rehab program, continue with regular exercise through daily

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: activities such as light yard and housework.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Other good exercise options with your doctor's approval are walking, swimming, and bicycling.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: As you exercise, keep these precautions in mind.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Rest as needed.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Stop if you feel chest pain or have shortness of breath.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: And don't exercise when temperatures are very hot or very cold.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Stress can affect your blood pressure and your ability to maintain a healthy lifestyle.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Identifying the causes of your stress and learning to eliminate or manage them can help

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: with your recovery.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: You may feel depressed.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Just these feelings with your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: He or she can prescribe medicine, counseling, or a support group to help you adjust.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: It is important to stay aware of changes in your symptoms and know when to get help.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: If your hospital stay included a procedure with an incision, check the site each day for

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: any signs of infection.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Call your doctor if you develop a fever or redness, heat, pain, tenderness, pus, or bleeding

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: at the incision site.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Be mindful of any symptoms of chest pain, pressure, tightness, heaviness, ache, or any other

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: kind of unusual sensation in your chest as any of these could mean you have a blockage

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: in the blood vessels of the heart.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Symptoms related to blockage in the blood vessels of the heart are called angina and could

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: be any one of these symptoms.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Angina symptoms may also be felt in your arms, shoulders, neck, jaw, throat, or back.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Call your doctor if you think you're experiencing angina.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Call 911 immediately if you have any of these symptoms.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Angina that doesn't go away with medication is prescribed by your doctor.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Shortness of breath.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Cold sweats.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: nausea.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Vomiting.

file: AND14017_After a Heart Attack_ Managing Your Recovery.mp4
title: AND14017 After a Heart Attack  Managing Your Recovery
text_en: Or lightheadedness.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Please watch the entire video before giving an anti-coagulant shot.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: This video will teach you how to give yourself a shot of anti-coagulant medicine, also called a blood thinner.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Blood thinners help prevent clots from forming inside your blood vessels.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Before you begin, you will need alcohol wipes, your pre-filled syringe of anti-coagulant medicine,

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: and a sharp spot.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: The main parts of the syringe are the barrel containing the medicine, the plunger, the needle cap, and the needle.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Select your injection site at least two inches to the right or left of your belly button or on either of your thighs.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: A caregiver may also use these instructions to give you a shot on either side of your back, upper arms, or upper buttocks.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Use a different injection site each day to avoid swelling or bruising.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: And take your shot at the same time each day.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Be sure to follow your doctor's instructions. Do not skip any doses.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Do not inject more medicine than prescribed.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 1. Before you begin, wash your hands with soap and water and then dry them.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 2. Clean the skin over the injection site by rubbing with an alcohol wipe in a circular motion.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 3. To open your syringe, peel the foil cover off the back.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Do not push the syringe through the foil cover because it could damage the syringe.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 4. Hold the syringe sideways by the barrel. Then, pull the needle cap straight off without bending the needle.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: There will be an air bubble in the medicine. Do not press the plunger to squirt the air bubble out of the barrel.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 5. With your free hand, pinch and hold an inch of skin at the injection site.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Insert the needle at a 90-degree angle through your skin and into the fatty tissue beneath it.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 6. Slowly press the plunger to deliver all of the medicine, including the air bubble.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 7. Pull the needle straight out of your skin.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 8. Press down firmly on the plunger to activate the safety guard which covers the needle.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Step 9. Throw away the used syringe in your sharp spots.

file: AND16019_How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot.mp4
title: AND16019 How To Give An Anticoagulant Shot
text_en: Call your doctor if you notice increased bleeding or bruising, rash, numbness or tingling, especially in your legs.

title: AND23020 Managing Heart Failure
text_en: If you have been diagnosed with heart failure, it's important to control your symptoms.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: If you are having an operation, you may be given general anesthesia to push you to sleep

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: and keep you free from pain.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Your doctor may recommend general anesthesia for a procedure that is extensive, takes a long

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: time, or requires you to be in an uncomfortable position.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Before your procedure, an IV line will be placed in a vein in your arm using a small tube

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: called a cannula.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The IV will deliver fluid and medications directly into your bloodstream.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You may receive some medication to help you relax.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be placed on the operating table and made as comfortable as possible.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: A blood pressure cuff will be placed on your arm to check your blood pressure readings.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Sticky pads will be placed on your chest to check your heart rate.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: And a clip will be put on your finger to check your body's oxygen levels.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: These devices allow the anesthesia specialist to closely monitor your vital signs before,

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: during and after your procedure.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will begin receiving general anesthesia by either breathing anesthetic gases through

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: a mask or through IV injection, which will cause you to fall asleep.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Once you are asleep, you will be given a mixture of oxygen and anesthetic gases either

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: through your mask or through a special tube inserted through your mouth and into your windpipe.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The tube is attached to a respirator, which helps you breathe while delivering the gases

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: to your lungs.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Deep in your lung tissues, the gases are absorbed into your bloodstream and carried by blood

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: cells to your brain.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: The anesthesia prevents your brain from receiving messages from nerves in your body.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: As a result, you will remain asleep and pain-free during your procedure, and you will have no

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: memory of it when you wake up.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: After your operation, the anesthesia specialist will give you medications to reverse the anesthesia,

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: and you will awaken quickly.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: If you had a breathing tube in place, he or she will remove it as soon as you can safely

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: breathe on your own.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You will be taken to the recovery room where you will be closely monitored, and given

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: pain medication is needed.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: You may feel light-headed and slightly disoriented, but this feeling should pass quickly.

title: ANH00007 General Anesthesia
text_en: Once you are stable, you will be sent to a hospital room or home.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: A surgeon will insert a pacemaker into your body if your heart's conduction system,

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: the electrical system that makes your heart beat, is not working properly.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The heart is a muscular organ with four chambers.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Blood collects in the heart's two upper chambers or atria before filling the two lower

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: chambers or ventricles.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: In the ventricles contract, blood in the right ventricle is pumped to the lungs and blood

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: in the left ventricle is pumped to the rest of the body.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The heart has a natural pacemaker called the Sinoatrial node or SA node.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The pacemaker controls the heart rate by precisely determining when the muscles of the atria

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: and ventricles contract.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: The electrical signal produced by the SA node reaches the ventricles through a narrow pathway

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: that includes the so-called AV node and bundle branches.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Taken together, these pathways are referred to as the conduction system of the heart.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Problems with the conduction system can result if the SA node malfunctions, causing the

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: heart to beat too fast, too slow or irregularly.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Alternatively, a blockage in the bundle branches can develop, causing the heart to beat more

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: slowly or irregularly.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: These problems can be due to heart disease, medications, or cardiac surgery.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: Before the procedure, your blood and urine will be tested.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: You will also have x-rays taken of your chest, and an electrocardiogram or EKG will record

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
text_en: the electrical activity in your heart.

title: ANH00010 Pacemaker Insertion
